preFix.title: Two-colour sewn headband
preFix.subtitle: Sewing a striped decorative headband onto the head and tail of a text block
preFix.excerpt: A hand-sewn headband uses two alternating threads to produce a horizontal stripe along the top and bottom of a book's spine. The structure protects the top of the text block and becomes decorative on an exposed-spine binding.
preFix.sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapters on headbands and endbands. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/bookbindingandca00cock. Cockerell gives the sewing sequence for both single- and two-colour headbands.

content.text: A
headband
sewn in two contrasting colours makes the stripe pattern by alternating which thread wraps over the
core

at each stitch. Thread A wraps and ties into the text block; thread B does nothing. On the next stitch they swap. The result is a horizontal spiral of alternating colour visible from outside the spine when the book is shelved. Douglas Cockerell recommended hand-sewn headbands for any book where the spine would be visible, noting that the stuck-on commercial headband "serves no structural purpose whatever".
What you need

items.name: Bookbinding thread, linen (waxed), in two colours

content.text: The core is typically a length of medium-weight cord or a tightly rolled strip of cartridge paper about 3 mm in diameter. The cord sits along the top edge of the spine with the thread winding around it. Cut the core to the exact width of the text block spine.
Preparing the text block
The text block must be sewn and the spine consolidated with a thin coat of acid-free PVA, dried to a tacky state. The headband sews through the top two or three signatures; these must be accessible.
Pierce three
tie-down stitch
holes through the topmost signature: one at each end of the spine and one in the middle. These are the anchoring points.
Sewing the headband
Thread the needle with colour A. Tie the thread to the core at the left end with two half-hitches. Leave the core resting along the head edge of the text block.
Loop thread A over the core from back to front. Pass the needle into the left tie-down hole and pull through. This creates the first visible stitch on the outer face of the spine.
Bring colour B to the core. Loop thread B over the core from back to front on the next position, one stitch width to the right of the first. Pull the thread snug.

Alternate A and B across the full width of the spine, working left to right. Every three or four stitches, pass the working thread through the next tie-down hole to anchor the headband to the text block. A headband without tie-down stitches will slide off the spine over time.
At the right end, tie both threads off behind the core with a square knot. Trim the ends to 5 mm and tuck behind the outer layer of the headband with the tip of the bookbinding needle.
Repeat at the tail of the text block with a second headband.
Common mistakes

items.fix: Use a pencil to mark equally spaced stitch positions along the top edge of the text block before starting. Space them 2 to 3 mm apart depending on thread weight.
items.cause: Inconsistent tension or unequal stitch spacing.
items.symptom: The stripe is uneven; some stitches are wider or narrower than others

items.fix: Add at least three tie-down stitches for an A5 spine. For a longer spine, space them every 12 to 15 mm.
items.cause: Tie-down stitches were omitted or too sparse.
items.symptom: The headband rolls or slides on the spine after finishing

items.fix: Use high-contrast colours. Natural undyed linen with a red or blue thread reads clearly. Two similar tan tones merge at normal reading distance.
items.cause: The threads are too similar in colour or weight.
items.symptom: Colour A and B are not clearly distinct on the finished headband
